example_name: TikTok GDPR Fine 2020
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission examined TikTok's data practices concerning children's personal data. The platform, used by millions globally, collected data including location, device information, and behavioral data from users under 16 without proper age verification or parental consent. This raised issues about processing personal data on a massive scale through mobile apps.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Dutch DPA fined TikTok €725,000 for privacy violations. TikTok adjusted its age gates and default privacy settings across Europe.

example_name: Facebook GDPR Fine by ICO
example_background: Following the Cambridge Analytica scandal, the ICO examined Facebook's data processing practices. Between 2007 and 2014, an app developer accessed data of up to 87 million users worldwide, including 4 million EU citizens, without adequate consent mechanisms. Facebook's platform allowed third-party apps to harvest data beyond what users explicitly permitted, and the company failed to enforce consistent data protection standards across its global operations.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019, the maximum penalty under pre-GDPR law but applied under GDPR transition. Facebook implemented changes to its data-sharing policies and third-party app reviews.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Schrems II Compliance
example_background: Following the Schrems II court ruling in 2020, Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook) adjusted its data transfers from the EU to the US. The company implemented new contractual safeguards and encryption measures to ensure equivalent protection levels for EU personal data processed in the US, addressing concerns over US surveillance laws and promoting consistent data protection standards.
process_and_outcome: Meta adopted the EU-US Data Privacy Framework in 2023 and supplemented it with Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and additional measures. Irish DPC confirmed compliance in ongoing audits, avoiding further penalties post-initial fines.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: Whisticall GDPR Compliance Program
example_background: Whisticall, a UK telecom provider, developed a comprehensive GDPR compliance framework post-2018 to ensure uniform data protection across its operations. This included data mapping, privacy by design in call recording systems, and staff training. The company maintained detailed records of processing activities and conducted regular DPIAs for marketing and customer service data processing, aligning with EU-wide standards.
process_and_outcome: ICO audit in 2020 confirmed full compliance, with no enforcement actions. Whisticall received certification under the ICO's Accountability Framework, demonstrating effective implementation.

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine by Hamburg DPA
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary monitored employee conversations in its call center and back office from 2014 to 2018, recording details about personal lives, health, and salaries. This data was stored in an 'employee data book' shared among managers without employee knowledge or consent. The practices involved processing sensitive personal data inconsistently with GDPR standards across EU operations.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M €35.3 million in October 2020. H&M discontinued the practice, implemented new data protection policies, and compensated affected employees.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Austria Data Retention Law Invalidation
example_background: In 2014, the Austrian Constitutional Court examined national legislation that implemented EU data retention directives by requiring telecommunications providers to retain traffic and location data for six months. The court assessed whether this transposition aligned with EU law requirements, including those outlined in GDPR recitals concerning member state specifications. The law had been adopted to enable law enforcement access for serious crime investigations, but concerns arose over the proportionality and necessity of blanket retention without sufficient safeguards. The court reviewed the compatibility with fundamental rights, data minimization principles, and the framework for national incorporation of EU rules. This case highlighted tensions between EU-wide standards and national implementation choices, particularly how member states integrated retention obligations into domestic frameworks while attempting to maintain coherence with GDPR's emphasis on targeted measures.
process_and_outcome: The Constitutional Court annulled sections of the Austrian Telecommunications Act on data retention in April 2014, finding disproportionate interference with privacy rights. The decision required revision of national laws, leading to the adoption of more targeted retention rules in 2018 that complied with CJEU precedents. No fines were imposed, but providers adjusted data handling practices.

example_name: German Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) Revision
example_background: Germany incorporated GDPR elements into its national law through the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG), particularly after the 2018 GDPR enforcement began. Sections of the BDSG specified rules for employment data processing, video surveillance in public areas, and data transfers for credit checks, providing restrictions and specifications as allowed under GDPR. This transposition aimed to ensure comprehensibility for German citizens and businesses by integrating EU provisions into familiar national legal structures. The process involved parliamentary debates on balancing GDPR's general rules with sector-specific needs, such as allowing employers to process employee health data under strict conditions for occupational safety. The law was updated multiple times to reflect CJEU rulings and maintain coherence.
process_and_outcome: The BDSG was successfully amended in 2017 and 2021 to fully transpose GDPR, passing constitutional review. It has been upheld in multiple administrative challenges, enabling consistent application without major enforcement disruptions.

example_name: French CNIL Enforcement Against Google Analytics
example_background: In 2022, the French data protection authority investigated the use of Google Analytics by websites in France, focusing on data transfers to the US. National law under the French Data Protection Act incorporated GDPR provisions on international transfers, specifying requirements for adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses. The case involved multiple websites using the tool for audience measurement, where IP addresses were collected and sent to Google servers without sufficient safeguards post-Schrems II. This raised questions about how France's national implementation of GDPR Article 49 derogations and transfer restrictions interacted with EU-wide rules.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued formal notices in June 2022 to stop processing data via Google Analytics within one month, citing inadequate transfer protections. Several providers suspended use, and the decision prompted EU-wide guidance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: French Data Protection Act (Loi Informatique et Libertés)

example_name: Irish DPC TikTok Children's Data Fine
example_background: TikTok's practices regarding children's data in the EU came under scrutiny, with Ireland's Data Protection Commission leading the investigation. Irish law supplements GDPR through the Data Protection Act 2018, which incorporates EU rules on child consent and specifies age verification thresholds. The probe examined age gating, behavioral advertising targeting minors, and default public settings for under-16 accounts from 2018-2020, assessing how national provisions on parental consent aligned with GDPR's special protections for children.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million for mishandling children's data, following a multi-year investigation with input from other EU regulators under the EDPB cooperation mechanism.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Amazon Fine
example_background: Spain's data protection authority examined Amazon's behavioral advertising practices. The Spanish Organic Law on Data Protection (LOPDGDD) transposes GDPR by adding specifications for consent in digital services and restrictions on profiling. The case focused on Amazon's processing of user data for targeted ads without granular consent options, pre-ticked boxes, and dark patterns in the Spanish market from 2018 onwards.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, AEPD imposed a €2.5 million sanction on Amazon for transparency and consent failures, upheld after appeal considerations.

example_name: Netherlands National Biometrics Register Challenge
example_background: The Dutch government proposed a national facial recognition database for law enforcement, incorporating GDPR exceptions under Article 10 for criminal investigations. National law sought to specify biometric processing rules, but faced legal challenges over proportionality. Critics argued the transposition exceeded GDPR allowances by enabling mass scanning without sufficient national coherence.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Dutch court suspended the project, referring questions to the CJEU on GDPR compatibility, halting implementation pending EU clarification.
involved_parties: Dutch Ministry of Justice and Security

example_name: Italy's Data Protection Code Update
example_background: Italy updated its Legislative Decree 196/2003 (Privacy Code) in 2018 to incorporate GDPR, adding national specifications for journalistic processing, public administration data, and anti-spam rules. This ensured Italian-specific provisions were comprehensible while maintaining EU coherence, with provisions for simplified obligations for small entities.
process_and_outcome: The updated code was implemented without major challenges, supporting smooth GDPR transition and referenced positively in subsequent Garante decisions.

example_name: Belgium Employee Data Processing Guidelines
example_background: Belgium's data protection authority issued guidelines incorporating GDPR into employment law, specifying rules for monitoring employee emails, CCTV, and performance tracking. The national implementation via the Collective Bargaining Agreement No. 81 provided detailed restrictions to ensure clarity for employers and workers.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines adopted in 2020 have been applied in compliance audits, reducing disputes and cited in successful defenses against claims.

example_name: Portugal Facial Recognition in Schools Suspension
example_background: Portuguese schools implemented facial recognition for attendance tracking, relying on national law specifications under GDPR for educational purposes. The setup processed biometric data of minors without explicit parental opt-in, prompting review of how Portugal's transposition handled sensitive data in public sector contexts.
process_and_outcome: CNPD ordered suspension in 2020, fining the provider €400,000 for lack of legal basis and DPIA failures, leading to system dismantling.

example_name: Facebook (Meta) GDPR Enforcement by Irish DPC
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Facebook's processing of personal data from EU users, focusing on transfers to the US following the Schrems II ruling. Facebook relied on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for cross-border data flows between EU member states and the US, involving billions of users' data shared across public and private entities. The inquiry examined compliance with cooperation requirements among national authorities.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision leading to a €1.2 billion fine against Meta Platforms Ireland Limited. The company was ordered to suspend transfers and bring data back to the EU, with ongoing appeals.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: OneStopPlus Data Breach
example_background: In 2022, German online retailer Otto Group’s subsidiary OneStopPlus experienced a cyber-attack exposing personal data of around 1.6 million customers. The incident involved data processed across EU borders, shared between group companies in multiple member states, affecting customers in Germany, France, and other countries.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner investigated and fined the company €105,000 in 2023 for inadequate security measures and delayed breach notification.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Schrems I and II Cases
example_background: Austrian privacy activist Max Schrems challenged Facebook's data transfers from the EU to the US, where data of EU users was processed on US servers. The cases addressed the adequacy of safeguards for cross-border data flows between EU member states and third countries, involving cooperation among national data protection authorities.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated the EU-US Safe Harbor framework in 2015 (Schrems I) and Privacy Shield in 2020 (Schrems II), requiring stricter protections for international data transfers.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: EU Law Enforcement Data Exchange (SIENA System)
example_background: The Secure Information Exchange Network Application (SIENA) is used by EU member states' law enforcement authorities to exchange personal data securely for cross-border investigations, such as terrorism and organized crime cases. It facilitates cooperation as required by EU law, processing data on behalf of authorities in other states.
process_and_outcome: Implemented since 2001, SIENA has handled millions of messages annually, enabling efficient data exchanges while adhering to data protection standards. Regular audits ensure compliance.
involved_parties: National law enforcement authorities in EU Member States

example_name: Meta GDPR Transfers to US Fine
example_background: In 2023, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's transfer of EU users' personal data to the US under the Privacy Shield, post-Schrems II ruling. Data including behavioral profiles for advertising was transferred without adequate safeguards against US surveillance laws, processing vast amounts of data for commercial purposes.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board instructed a €1.2 billion fine on Meta in May 2023. Meta suspended transfers and is developing new mechanisms like standard contractual clauses with supplementary measures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: Apple GDPR Compliance in App Tracking Transparency
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5 in 2021, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking across apps and websites via the IDFA identifier. This framework balanced user privacy rights with developers' interests in advertising, providing clear information and opt-in mechanisms for data processing.
process_and_outcome: The feature has been upheld in regulatory reviews with no major fines, leading to widespread adoption and billions of opt-outs. It set an industry standard praised for enhancing user control.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Recital 4 (Balance with freedom to conduct business)

example_name: ProtonMail GDPR Compliance for Encrypted Services
example_background: ProtonMail provides end-to-end encrypted email services based in Switzerland, with EU operations ensuring data minimization by design. Personal data processing is limited to essentials, with zero-access encryption preventing even Proton from accessing user content, balancing privacy with service functionality.
process_and_outcome: Proton has faced no GDPR fines and is recognized in audits for strong compliance, serving millions while maintaining transparency reports and open-source code.
involved_parties: Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner

example_name: H&M Data Protection Violation
example_background: In 2020, the Hamburg data protection authority investigated H&M's service center in Nuremberg, Germany, where employee gossip and private details about colleagues' personal lives—such as health issues, family matters, and vacation experiences—were collected, stored, and shared among managers via a shared drive. This affected hundreds of employees over several years.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M Europe €35.25 million in October 2020. The company cooperated during the investigation and discontinued the practice, introducing new data protection training.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: OneTrust GDPR Compliance Framework Implementation
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, developed and implemented a comprehensive GDPR compliance platform used by thousands of organizations across the EU. The platform facilitates data mapping, consent management, DPIAs, and breach reporting, addressing fragmentation by providing standardized tools adaptable to national variations in Directive 95/46/EC implementation. Adopted widely post-GDPR, it helped companies harmonize practices amid legal uncertainties.
process_and_outcome: Numerous clients achieved GDPR certification and avoided penalties through OneTrust's tools. The company received industry recognition for enabling compliance successes, with case studies showing reduced risk exposure.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors GDPR Readiness Program
example_background: SAP, a major enterprise software provider, launched a GDPR readiness program for its SuccessFactors HR cloud platform ahead of May 2018. The program included data inventory tools, pseudonymization features, automated DPIAs, and cross-border transfer mechanisms compliant with varying national standards. This addressed pre-GDPR fragmentation by ensuring consistent protection levels for employee data processed across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: SAP customers successfully transitioned without major incidents, earning certifications like ISO 27701. The program was praised in industry reports for promoting uniform compliance.

example_name: SAP GDPR Accountability Measures
example_background: SAP implemented comprehensive GDPR compliance programs including data protection by design, regular privacy impact assessments, and transparent data processing records across its cloud services. The company provides customers with tools for data subject rights management and audit-ready documentation, ensuring legal and practical certainty for users of its enterprise software.
process_and_outcome: SAP's proactive measures have resulted in no major GDPR fines and recognition through certifications like ISO 27701. The approach has supported thousands of customers in achieving compliance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 25 (Data protection by design and by default)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland WhatsApp Fine 2021
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated WhatsApp's processing of user data for targeted advertising. Following Facebook's policy changes, user data was shared across its services without sufficient transparency or legal basis. Metadata including contacts, device information, and usage patterns for over 500 million EU users was involved.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp Ireland €225 million. The decision resulted from a GDPR investigation initiated in 2018. WhatsApp updated its privacy policy and data-sharing mechanisms.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 14 (Information where data not obtained from data subject)

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Fine by CNIL 2023
example_background: CNIL analyzed Microsoft's handling of advertising cookies on Bing search engine and LinkedIn. Cookies were deposited without prior user consent, and the cookie banner lacked a clear refusal option equivalent to acceptance. The practices affected user tracking across EU websites.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, CNIL fined Microsoft Ireland Operations €60 million. Orders required implementation of compliant cookie management. Microsoft updated its consent mechanisms.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Hamburg DPA Fine on DPO
example_background: In 2022, the Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information fined a Data Protection Officer (DPO) of a political party €100,000 for influencing the party's data processing practices to circumvent GDPR obligations. The DPO, tasked with ensuring compliance, instead advised on methods to process membership data without adequate legal basis or transparency, including bulk data transfers and profiling without consent.
process_and_outcome: The authority determined the DPO violated independence requirements and imposed the fine directly on the individual. The party ceased the non-compliant practices.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Schrems II Case - EU-US Data Transfers
example_background: The case involved challenges to the Privacy Shield framework, which facilitated personal data transfers between the EU and the US. Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, argued that US surveillance programs under laws such as Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333 enabled access to EU citizens' data by US intelligence agencies without adequate safeguards. This raised concerns about the applicability of EU data protection rules to transatlantic data flows where national security exceptions in the US could override individual rights. The Court of Justice of the EU examined whether such surveillance activities fell outside the scope of EU law due to national security exemptions similar to those in GDPR Recital 16.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the CJEU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and required stricter scrutiny of Standard Contractual Clauses. The decision highlighted that national security processing must still respect EU fundamental rights where EU law applies, leading to increased use of additional safeguards in data transfer agreements.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: Austrian DSB Ruling on Google Analytics
example_background: The Austrian Data Protection Authority examined a complaint against a website operator using Google Analytics, focusing on IP address transfers to the US. It found that US surveillance practices under laws permitting bulk data access by national security agencies did not provide equivalent protection to GDPR standards, even considering tool modifications like IP anonymization. The decision underscored limits on data transfers where recipient countries' national security regimes impinge on EU data flows.
process_and_outcome: In January 2022, the DSB declared the use unlawful, leading the website operator to disable Analytics. This contributed to a wave of EU-wide scrutiny on similar tracking technologies.

example_name: EU Commission Adequacy Decision for Japan
example_background: The European Commission assessed Japan's data protection framework, including its national security exceptions under the Act on the Protection of Personal Information. Japan implemented supplementary rules ensuring that any public authority access for security purposes was limited and subject to safeguards, demonstrating alignment with EU standards despite Recital 16-like exemptions. This enabled personal data flows without additional transfer mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: In January 2019, the Commission adopted an adequacy decision, renewed in 2024 after review, allowing seamless data transfers. Regular joint reviews monitor compliance with security-related processing.

example_name: UK ICO Guidance on National Security Exemptions
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office provided detailed guidance on when processing personal data for national security purposes falls outside GDPR scope, mirroring EU Recital 16. It outlined scenarios such as intelligence services using data for counter-terrorism, emphasizing that such activities by competent authorities are exempt but must still adhere to human rights obligations where applicable.
process_and_outcome: Issued in 2020 and updated post-Brexit under UK GDPR, the guidance has been used in compliance assessments, with no major enforcement actions against exempt entities reported.

example_name: Swedish IMY Decision on Police Data Processing
example_background: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection reviewed a case where police processed personal data for national security investigations. The authority confirmed that such processing by law enforcement fell outside standard GDPR rules due to specific exemptions for activities ensuring public security, aligning with the regulation's carve-outs for national security.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, IMY ruled the processing compliant with exemptions, dismissing complaints and clarifying boundaries between applicable and non-applicable scopes.

example_name: CJEU Tele2 Sverige Case
example_background: The case concerned Swedish legislation requiring telecom providers to retain metadata for national security purposes, accessible by intelligence services. The CJEU assessed whether such generalized retention and access fell within EU law scope or was excluded, balancing data protection with security needs under frameworks predating GDPR.
process_and_outcome: In 2016, the CJEU ruled generalized retention disproportionate, requiring targeted measures. This influenced post-GDPR national security laws to incorporate stricter limits.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Dutch DPA Ruling on Military Data Processing
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority investigated personal data handling by the Ministry of Defence for intelligence and security operations. It determined that processing related to national defence and state security activities was excluded from GDPR oversight, as these fell under national security exceptions.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the DPA closed the investigation without findings of non-compliance, affirming the exemption's application.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Dutch Intelligence and Security Services Act 2017

example_name: Bavarian DPA Confirmation on Border Control Data
example_background: The Bavarian data protection authority handled queries on personal data processing by border police for security checks. It clarified that such activities, involving EU citizens' data for preventing threats, were outside GDPR scope when conducted under national security mandates.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the authority issued a decision upholding the exemption, providing guidance for similar public sector processing.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision

example_name: European Parliament Data Breach 2017
example_background: In October 2017, the European Parliament experienced a data breach involving the personal data of Members of the European Parliament (MEPs), parliamentary assistants, and other staff. The incident stemmed from the use of an outdated version of Drupal on the Parliament's website, which contained a known vulnerability exploited by attackers. Approximately 1.5 GB of data was accessed, including names, email addresses, photos, and political group affiliations. The breach was discovered during routine monitoring, and it highlighted issues in the timely application of security patches to systems processing personal data within EU institutions. The Parliament's data protection framework at the time was governed by Regulation (EC) No 45/2001, which set rules for data processing by EU bodies but had not yet been fully aligned with newer standards. This event occurred amid broader discussions on strengthening data protection across the EU, prompting internal reviews of IT infrastructure and patch management processes used by EU institutions.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) conducted an investigation and issued recommendations for improved vulnerability management and incident response. No fines were imposed as it involved EU institutions, but the Parliament implemented enhanced security measures, including regular audits and updates to its content management systems. A public report detailed the findings and remedial actions taken.

example_name: EDPS Inquiry into EU Institutions' Data Processing 2014
example_background: In 2014, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) launched an inquiry into the processing of personal data by EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies under Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. The review examined practices such as data retention periods, access controls, and data sharing between institutions like the European Commission and Council. It identified inconsistencies in how personal data of EU staff, citizens filing complaints, and third parties was handled, including excessive retention and inadequate anonymization. This occurred prior to the full enforcement of GDPR, underscoring the need for adaptations to align with emerging principles like data minimization and accountability. The inquiry involved on-site inspections and document reviews across multiple EU bodies.
process_and_outcome: The EDPS published a report with 20 recommendations, leading to policy updates, training programs, and the establishment of data protection officers in more units. EU institutions committed to implementing changes by 2016, with follow-up audits confirming partial compliance improvements.

example_name: European Commission Employee Data Processing Issues 2019
example_background: Following the GDPR's entry into application in 2018, the European Data Protection Supervisor investigated the European Commission's handling of employee personal data in its HR management systems. The case involved processing of sensitive data such as health information for sick leave management without fully updated legal bases or privacy notices, relying on legacy practices from Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. Data was stored in centralized databases accessible by multiple departments, raising concerns over purpose limitation and access rights. This reflected transitional challenges as EU institutions adapted their internal data processing rules to GDPR principles.
process_and_outcome: The EDPS issued a formal decision requiring the Commission to revise its data processing operations, update privacy statements, and conduct data protection impact assessments. Compliance was achieved through remediation plans monitored over 12 months, with no further enforcement actions.

example_name: EDPS Approval of EU Institutions' GDPR Adaptation 2021
example_background: In response to GDPR Recital 17, EU institutions worked on adapting Regulation (EC) No 45/2001, culminating in the proposal for Regulation (EU) 2022/2094 (EUDPR). The EDPS reviewed and endorsed the adaptation process, which included harmonizing definitions, strengthening data subject rights, and introducing mandatory data protection impact assessments for high-risk processing within institutions like the European Central Bank and EU agencies. This effort ensured coherent application across all EU bodies, with pilot implementations in data sharing platforms for staff mobility and public consultations.
process_and_outcome: The new EUDPR was adopted in October 2022, repealing Regulation 45/2001, with the EDPS confirming successful alignment. Institutions received guidance for transition, leading to standardized privacy frameworks without disruptions.

example_name: European Ombudsman Investigation into EU Data Practices 2016
example_background: The European Ombudsman investigated complaints regarding the processing of personal data by EU institutions in public access to documents requests. Cases involved the European Commission's disclosure of personal data without proper redaction or consent checks under Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. Data included contact details of individuals in policy consultations, processed without fully accounting for proportionality and necessity principles that GDPR later emphasized. The probe covered multiple institutions and highlighted gaps in data protection during transparency operations.
process_and_outcome: The Ombudsman issued findings recommending better balancing of transparency and data protection, leading to updated guidelines and training. Institutions adjusted procedures, reducing improper disclosures in subsequent years.

example_name: EDPS Guidance on Video Surveillance in EU Institutions 2018
example_background: The EDPS issued guidance following reviews of video surveillance systems deployed by EU institutions such as the European Parliament and Council for security purposes. These systems processed biometric and location data of staff and visitors under Regulation (EC) No 45/2001, with concerns over insufficient retention limits and signage. The guidance was part of adapting practices to GDPR standards, promoting privacy by design in institutional CCTV deployments.
process_and_outcome: Institutions adopted the guidance, implementing shorter retention periods, enhanced notices, and DPIAs. Follow-up audits by EDPS confirmed widespread adherence, serving as a model for compliance.

example_name: European Central Bank Staff Data Processing Review 2020
example_background: The EDPS reviewed the European Central Bank's processing of employee personal data in performance evaluation and wellness programs. Legacy systems under Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 lacked updated consent mechanisms and data minimization for health data, prompting a transition to GDPR-aligned tools during the adaptation phase referenced in Recital 17.
process_and_outcome: EDPS recommended and verified updates to legal bases and access controls. The ECB implemented a new HR data platform, achieving full compliance certification.

example_name: Implementation of EUDPR in EU Agencies 2023
example_background: Post-adoption of Regulation (EU) 2022/2094 adapting Regulation (EC) No 45/2001, EU agencies like the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) rolled out new data protection policies. This included mandatory DPOs, annual privacy training, and integrated record of processing activities, demonstrating proactive alignment with GDPR principles across decentralized bodies.
process_and_outcome: EDPS conducted initial compliance checks, confirming successful transitions with no major issues. Agencies published transparency reports showcasing adherence.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Fine
example_background: Google Ireland Limited provided Google Analytics services to the Schleswig-Holstein state geological survey in Germany. The service involved transferring personal data of German users to the United States without adequate safeguards. Data included IP addresses, browser identifiers, and device information collected via cookies. The state authority used the tool for website analytics from 2020 to 2022. Concerns arose over the lack of appropriate transfer mechanisms under GDPR chapter V, given the US does not ensure an adequate level of protection.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Authority conducted an investigation and, in 2024, imposed a fine of €15 million on Google Ireland. The decision highlighted insufficient measures for data transfers outside the EU. Google announced plans to contest the ruling.
involved_parties: Landesamt für Bergbau, Energie und Geologie (LBEG)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland WhatsApp Fine
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited processed personal data of approximately 500 million EU users for advertising purposes. The processing involved combining data from WhatsApp with data from other Meta services like Facebook and Instagram without providing a sufficient legal basis. This occurred between 2018 and 2021, affecting user interactions, device information, and metadata. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined the practices following complaints from privacy organizations.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the Irish DPC imposed a fine of €225 million on WhatsApp. The decision was part of a coordinated EU-wide investigation by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). WhatsApp adjusted its processing activities in response.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Fine 2018
example_background: British Airways experienced a data breach between August and September 2018, where personal data of approximately 400,000 customers was accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's payment processing system. The breach involved the harvesting of credit card details, names, addresses, and other personal information of individuals booking flights or holidays. The incident occurred due to a Magecart-style attack exploiting a security flaw, and the company's detection and response were delayed, allowing prolonged exposure. This event drew significant public and regulatory attention as it affected a large number of natural persons across Europe, highlighting issues in securing customer data during online transactions for a major airline operator.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the breach, initially proposing a fine of £183 million, which was later reduced to £20 million in October 2020 following an appeal and appeal withdrawal. British Airways implemented enhanced security measures, including improved monitoring and vulnerability management, as part of remediation efforts.

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Fine 2018-2019
example_background: Marriott International, through its Starwood Hotels & Resorts subsidiary, suffered a data breach affecting up to 383 million guests worldwide from 2014 to 2018, discovered in September 2018. Attackers accessed the Starwood guest reservation database, extracting names, passport numbers, payment card details, travel dates, and other personal data of natural persons. The breach persisted undetected for four years due to inadequate security controls post the 2016 acquisition of Starwood by Marriott, with insufficient integration of IT systems and monitoring. Notification to affected individuals occurred months later.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a £18.4 million fine in November 2020 after investigation. Marriott also faced a €14.7 million fine from the CNIL in France and ongoing class action lawsuits in the US, leading to enhanced cybersecurity protocols and compensation programs for affected guests.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Fine by CNIL 2022
example_background: The French data protection authority investigated Google's use of Google Analytics on websites, finding that the tool transferred personal data of French users to the US without adequate safeguards. The processing involved IP addresses, browser identifiers, and behavioral data of natural persons browsing sites using the service. Despite Standard Contractual Clauses, the transfers were deemed invalid due to US surveillance laws accessing the data, affecting transparency and consent mechanisms for millions of users.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €150 million fine on Google Ireland in 2022, the largest under GDPR at the time, ordering cessation of non-compliant transfers. Google adjusted its services and faced similar probes in other EU countries.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fine by Irish DPC 2023
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission examined Meta's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising on Facebook, involving billions of natural persons' data points like interests, locations, and interactions. The inquiry focused on whether consents were freely given and if data was processed beyond stated purposes, with findings of non-compliance in legal bases for processing across EU users since GDPR's inception.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the Irish DPC fined Meta Platforms Ireland €265 million, with additional investigations ongoing. Meta appealed and modified its advertising model, introducing consent or legitimate interest options.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine by Luxembourg DPA 2021
example_background: Amazon's advertising practices involved processing vast amounts of personal data from natural persons using its services, including purchase history, search queries, and locations for targeted ads. The Luxembourg regulator found insufficient transparency in how data was combined and used for profiling, lacking clear information on legal bases and purposes.
process_and_outcome: CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in July 2021, the largest GDPR fine then. Amazon appealed to EU courts, arguing the decision lacked merit, and adjusted its consent banners.
involved_parties: CNPD (Luxembourg Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données)

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Fine by Dutch DPA 2022
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a biometric database, processing biometric data of natural persons without consent or legal basis. The service sold access to law enforcement and private entities, enabling facial recognition searches based on uploaded photos of individuals.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Clearview AI €30.5 million in September 2022, ordering cessation of unlawful processing. Multiple EU DPAs issued similar bans and fines, totaling over €50 million.

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine for Employee Data Processing 2023
example_background: H&M operated a 'welcome-back room' where returning employees' personal experiences, health data, and gossip were logged by managers and shared internally without consent. This processed sensitive data of natural persons (employees) in Germany, creating detailed profiles used in HR decisions, discovered via whistleblower reports.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2023 after investigation confirming systematic violations. H&M discontinued the practice and improved data handling policies.
involved_parties: Hamburg DPA (Der Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit)

example_name: Apple GDPR Compliance with App Tracking Transparency 2021
example_background: Apple introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework in iOS 14.5, requiring apps to obtain explicit user consent before tracking identifiers across apps and websites for advertising. This applied to personal data of natural persons using iPhones, providing granular control over IDFA sharing, with privacy nutrition labels detailing data practices.
process_and_outcome: No fines issued; the feature was praised by regulators like the Irish DPC for enhancing user rights. It led to industry-wide shifts, with billions of opt-out requests, demonstrating proactive adherence.

example_name: Swedish Police GDPR Processing Case
example_background: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection examined the Swedish Police Authority's practices regarding the processing of personal data in its databases. The police maintained extensive records including criminal intelligence on individuals, some of which involved data on persons not suspected of crimes but associated through relationships or locations. The processing occurred in systems used for crime prevention and investigation, raising questions about the applicability of general data protection rules versus specific law enforcement directives. The inquiry focused on whether certain data processing activities fell under the scope of the GDPR or were excluded due to their purpose related to criminal investigations.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection determined that the processing was governed by specific national laws transposing Directive (EU) 2016/680, thus falling outside the GDPR's direct application. No fine was imposed, and the police were instructed to ensure alignment with law enforcement data protection rules.

example_name: UK ICO Police National Database Decision
example_background: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) reviewed the Police National Database (PND), a system aggregating personal data from various police forces for purposes including crime prevention, detection, and prosecution. Concerns arose over the sharing and retention of data such as custody records, intelligence reports, and victim information across forces. The review assessed whether such processing by competent authorities was subject to GDPR or exempt under provisions for law enforcement activities.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued an audit report confirming that PND processing was primarily regulated under the Data Protection Act 2018, which incorporates Directive (EU) 2016/680, placing it outside GDPR's scope for core functions. Recommendations were made for improved governance, with no enforcement action taken.

example_name: Dutch Police Personal Data Breach Fine
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority investigated a data breach at the Dutch National Police where personal data of over 1,000 individuals, including sensitive information related to criminal investigations, was exposed online. The data included details from police registers used for detection and prosecution of offenses. The incident prompted scrutiny on whether the processing was under GDPR or law enforcement-specific rules.
process_and_outcome: The authority fined the police €225,000, applying GDPR provisions as the processing involved administrative tasks not exclusively tied to criminal investigation purposes, thus falling within GDPR scope despite the authority's competence.

example_name: German Federal Police BAMF Data Processing Fine
example_background: The Bavarian data protection authority examined the Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF) and Federal Police's handling of personal data in the context of border controls and migration management. Data processing included biometric and personal details for security checks, some linked to criminal offense prevention. The case involved prolonged storage and sharing of data beyond strict law enforcement needs.
process_and_outcome: A fine of €100,000 was imposed on BAMF for GDPR violations in data retention practices, as certain processing activities were deemed outside the pure scope of criminal prosecution under Directive 2016/680 and subject to GDPR.
involved_parties: Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF)
Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision

example_name: French CNIL Law Enforcement Data Audit
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) conducted an audit on the National Police's TAJ (Treatment of Criminal Antecedents) system, which processes personal data for criminal record management and investigation purposes. The system handles fingerprints, DNA, and judicial data for offense detection and prosecution across law enforcement bodies.
process_and_outcome: CNIL confirmed the processing's exemption from GDPR under Recital 19, governed instead by the Internal Security Code and Directive 2016/680. Controls were recommended to enhance compliance with law enforcement standards, with no sanctions applied.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Irish DPC An Garda Síochána PULSE System Review
example_background: Ireland's Data Protection Commission reviewed An Garda Síochána's PULSE system, a database for recording crimes, incidents, and personal data for investigation and prosecution purposes. Issues included data accuracy, retention periods, and access by personnel for public security tasks.
process_and_outcome: The DPC ruled that core PULSE operations for criminal matters were regulated under Part 4 of the Data Protection Act 2018 (transposing Directive 2016/680), excluding them from GDPR. Guidance was provided for non-law enforcement uses to comply with GDPR.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Police Data Sharing Case
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) investigated complaints about the National Police's sharing of personal data from criminal investigation files with private entities for debt collection, blurring lines between law enforcement processing and general data handling.
process_and_outcome: AEPD imposed a €0.7 million fine, determining that the sharing constituted processing under GDPR scope, not protected by the criminal prosecution exemption in Recital 19, as it served non-law enforcement purposes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Organic Law 3/2018 on Personal Data Protection

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Law Enforcement Processing
example_background: The European Data Protection Board issued guidelines clarifying the scope of GDPR versus the Law Enforcement Directive, using examples from member state practices where public authorities process data for both general and criminal purposes, such as municipal police handling traffic alongside crime data.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines 2/2020 provided framework distinguishing scopes, endorsing compliant delineations by authorities and influencing national implementations without specific enforcement.
involved_parties: EU Member State Data Protection Authorities

example_name: SMEs GDPR Record-Keeping Derogation Guidance
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued guidelines clarifying GDPR Article 30(5), which allows organizations with fewer than 250 employees to forgo maintaining detailed records of processing activities, provided they document data protection impact assessments and risks. This derogation aims to reduce administrative burdens for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) as defined by EU Recommendation 2003/361/EC, which sets thresholds for micro (<10 employees, turnover ≤€2M), small (<50, ≤€10M), and medium (<250, ≤€50M). SMEs must still ensure compliance with core principles but benefit from simplified obligations to promote market participation.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Guidelines 1/2021 adopted in May 2021 provide examples and best practices for SMEs to qualify and apply the derogation, emphasizing risk-based documentation. No enforcement actions tied directly, but used in supervisory guidance across EU.

example_name: ICO SME Data Protection Toolkit
example_background: The UK ICO developed a free online toolkit specifically tailored for small organizations, including those with fewer than 250 employees, to help them comply with GDPR/UK GDPR. The toolkit provides simplified templates for privacy notices, data protection policies, and breach response plans, taking into account the specific needs of SMEs as encouraged by Recital 13. It aligns with the record-keeping derogation while ensuring SMEs meet essential obligations like appointing a DPO if required or conducting DPIAs for high-risk processing.
process_and_outcome: Launched in 2018 and updated regularly, the toolkit has been adopted by thousands of UK SMEs, with ICO reporting improved compliance rates in audits of small businesses. No fines linked to toolkit users demonstrating adherence.

example_name: CNIL SME Compliance Support Initiative
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) launched a dedicated support program for SMEs and micro-enterprises, offering simplified guides, webinars, and diagnostic tools adapted to their size and resources. This reflects Recital 13's encouragement for supervisory authorities to consider SME needs, such as flexible record-keeping under Article 30(5) and proportionate DPIA requirements. The initiative covers sectors like retail and services where SMEs process employee and customer data.
process_and_outcome: Since 2018, CNIL has assisted over 10,000 SMEs, resulting in higher self-reported compliance and fewer minor infringements in audits. Integrated into France's digital strategy.
involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Record-Keeping Issues
example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) was found lacking adequate internal records of processing activities for behavioral advertising across EU users. The investigation revealed insufficient documentation of legal bases, retention periods, and risk assessments despite the company's scale. As a large enterprise with over 50,000 employees, Meta had no entitlement to the <250 employee derogation in Recital 13.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined €91 million in November 2023 following a 2020-2022 inquiry. Meta updated records and faced additional proceedings on targeted advertising.

example_name: Deloitte Croatia GDPR Fine
example_background: In 2019, the Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) examined Deloitte's processing practices. Deloitte maintained manual files and unstructured sets of paper documents containing personal data of employees and clients without organizing them according to specific criteria for retrieval. These files were not indexed or structured in a way that allowed systematic access, raising questions about whether they qualified as part of a 'filing system' under GDPR definitions.
process_and_outcome: AZOP imposed a fine of €200 on Deloitte d.o.o. for the administrative violation, determining that the unstructured manual files fell outside the strict scope of GDPR's filing system requirements but still required basic safeguards. This was one of the first cases interpreting the scope limitations in Recital 15.

example_name: Swedish Authority Data Breach Fine
example_background: In 2019, the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) investigated a data breach at a public authority where personal data was processed through both automated IT systems and manual paper-based filing systems. The breach involved unauthorized access to employee personal data stored in physical folders and digital records intended for inclusion in structured systems, affecting around 200 individuals with sensitive health and contact information.
process_and_outcome: IMY issued a fine of SEK 100,000 (approximately €9,500), emphasizing that manual processing within filing systems is subject to the same protections as automated processing. The authority implemented new procedures post-incident.

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine by German Authority
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center collected detailed personal information from employees on leave, including sensitive details about illnesses, private problems, and coping strategies, gathered through 'welcome back talks.' This data was stored in an electronic tool accessible to up to 50 managers without employees' knowledge or consent, violating data minimization and confidentiality principles.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision fined H&M JusticeCV €35.25 million in October 2020 after an employee complaint triggered the investigation.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (regulator)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fine for Facebook Pages
example_background: Meta Platforms operated Facebook, a platform where millions of users worldwide, including businesses and individuals, created and managed pages to share content, interact with audiences, and process data such as likes, comments, and user profiles. These pages involved systematic monitoring of EU data subjects' behavior across borders, enabling features like targeted advertising and audience insights. The processing occurred through Meta's infrastructure, which collected and analyzed personal data from page visitors and fans without individual user control over the data flows. This setup distinguished the platform's role from mere personal use by users, as Meta provided the technological means for data handling at scale, including data transfers to servers in the US. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined how page administrators' activities interfaced with Meta's backend systems, leading to extensive data processing beyond household contexts.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an investigation starting in 2018, culminating in a 2023 decision fining Meta Platforms Ireland Limited €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers. Meta was ordered to cease transfers and implement compliance measures within five months, with the company announcing plans to appeal the decision while complying with the suspension order.

example_name: WhatsApp Data Processing Fine
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Meta, provided end-to-end encrypted messaging for personal communications among EU users, including address book uploads and metadata collection for features like status updates and group chats. The service processed phone numbers, profile pictures, and usage patterns to operate the platform, distinguishing its infrastructure role from individual users' household messaging. In 2021, the Irish DPC investigated data sharing practices with Meta for advertising, revealing extensive processing of user data for commercial insights while claiming exemptions for personal use elements.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in 2021 for opaque privacy policies and unlawful processing, with the European Data Protection Board increasing it via dispute resolution. WhatsApp updated its policies and faced ongoing scrutiny.

example_name: Vimeo GDPR Enforcement for Video Platform
example_background: Vimeo operated a video hosting and sharing platform where users uploaded content for personal portfolios, family videos, or small creative projects. The service processed viewer IP addresses, watch times, and device data to deliver analytics and recommendations. EU users employed it for non-commercial household activities, but Vimo's US-based processing raised transfer issues. The CNIL examined whether Vimo's provision of analytics tools constituted regulated processing despite users' personal intent.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the French CNIL fined Vimeo €145 million (symbolic amount adjusted) and ordered cessation of US data transfers lacking safeguards, following findings of inadequate contractual protections.

example_name: Planet49 Cookie Consent Case
example_background: German company Webtrekk used Planet49's online quiz service on websites, pre-ticking consent boxes for cookies tracking user IDs and IP addresses for advertising. Users interacted in personal browsing contexts, but Planet49's service provided the data collection mechanism.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2019 that pre-ticked boxes do not constitute valid consent, influencing cookie practices across EU platforms.

example_name: WordPress.com Cookie Banner Fine
example_background: Automattic's WordPress.com hosted blogs where users shared personal content. The site's cookie banner obtained consent for analytics cookies processing visitor data, but the Hamburg DPA found the setup non-transparent for personal blogging platforms.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, Hamburg DPA fined €100,000 for invalid cookie consent mechanisms on the platform.

example_name: NRW.BANK LinkedIn Cookie Investigation
example_background: Germany's NRW.BANK embedded LinkedIn plugins on its career page, loading tracking cookies before consent during personal job searches by visitors. LinkedIn processed social network data via the bank site's infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Berlin DPA ruled in 2022 the practice unlawful without prior consent, requiring adjustments; no fine issued but formal findings.

example_name: Boris Schrems v. Facebook Fan Pages
example_background: Facebook fan pages operated by individuals or small entities processed visitor data via Facebook's pixel and insights tools for personal interest groups, with data handled by Meta's systems.
process_and_outcome: Austrian Court in 2025 referred questions to CJEU on whether fan pages fall under Recital 18 exemption, stemming from 2015-2022 litigation; ongoing.

example_name: Polish Courts Data Processing Investigation by SA
example_background: In 2018, the Polish Data Protection Authority (SA) received complaints regarding the processing of personal data by Polish courts in administrative proceedings. The complaints involved courts sharing personal data of complainants with third parties without proper legal basis, including details such as names, addresses, and case information. The SA initiated proceedings to assess whether the courts' actions in their judicial capacity fell under its competence, given GDPR Recital 20's provisions on judicial independence. The case highlighted tensions between data protection oversight and the autonomy of judicial bodies in handling case files and communications.
process_and_outcome: The SA determined it lacked competence to supervise the courts when acting in judicial capacity and dismissed the proceedings on January 30, 2019, referring complainants to judicial supervisory bodies. No fines were imposed, and the decision reinforced the scope limitations under GDPR Article 55(3).

example_name: Italian Garante Fine on Ministry of Justice Data Breach
example_background: In 2020, the Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) investigated a data breach at the Ministry of Justice involving the electronic platform for judicial notifications (PIE). The platform exposed personal data of over 18,000 lawyers, including tax codes, addresses, and phone numbers, due to inadequate access controls. The incident occurred during judicial notification processes managed by court personnel, raising questions about data security in judicial operations.
process_and_outcome: The Garante imposed a €1 million fine on the Ministry of Justice on July 16, 2020, for violations of data security principles. The authority asserted competence despite the judicial context, as the issue involved technical processing outside pure judicial decision-making.

example_name: UK ICO Guidance on Judicial Data Processing
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) issued guidance in 2021 clarifying that its oversight does not extend to courts acting in their judicial capacity, such as judges making decisions on personal data in cases. This was in response to inquiries about court handling of sensitive personal data in family and criminal proceedings, where data like victim identities and medical records are processed routinely.
process_and_outcome: ICO published non-binding guidance affirming exclusion of judicial functions from its competence, recommending judicial bodies establish internal compliance mechanisms. No enforcement actions were taken, serving as a best practice for respecting judicial independence.

example_name: French CNIL Decision on Court Data Processing
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority (CNIL) examined complaints against a tribunal for publishing full judgments online containing personal data of minors and sensitive health information without anonymization. The processing occurred as part of judicial decision dissemination on the Legifrance public database.
process_and_outcome: CNIL ruled on May 23, 2019, that it lacked competence over the judicial decision-making process itself but retained oversight for administrative aspects like website management. It issued formal notice for improved anonymization without fining, balancing oversight with independence.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Ruling on Judicial Data Breach
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) investigated a 2022 incident where a court in Madrid accidentally emailed personal data of 1,200 individuals involved in judicial proceedings to an unrelated third party. The data included full names, DNI numbers, and case details processed during administrative handling of court notifications.
process_and_outcome: AEPD imposed a €3,000 fine on the court administration on November 10, 2022, asserting competence over non-judicial processing activities. The decision distinguished between core judicial acts and support functions.

example_name: German Courts Internal Data Protection Officer Model
example_background: In Germany, the federal and state courts established internal data protection officers within the judicial system to oversee personal data processing in line with GDPR Recital 20. This includes training judges and staff on data minimization in case files and handling complaints about data use in proceedings, implemented since 2018 across various Länder courts.
process_and_outcome: The model has been recognized by the Bavarian Data Protection Authority as effective, with annual reports showing high compliance rates and resolution of over 500 complaints without external intervention, demonstrating proactive adherence.

example_name: Dutch Court Data Processing Complaint Dismissal
example_background: In 2021, a complaint was filed with the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) against a district court for retaining personal data in archived case files beyond stated retention periods, including biometric data from criminal cases.
process_and_outcome: AP dismissed the complaint on March 15, 2021, stating lack of competence under GDPR Article 55(3) for judicial archiving decisions, and directed the complainant to the court's internal oversight body.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Courts Service Breach
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) probed a 2023 cyber incident at the Courts Service of Ireland, where personal data from judicial IT systems, including civil case details, was accessed by unauthorized parties due to unpatched vulnerabilities.
process_and_outcome: DPC issued a reprimand and ordered remediation measures on September 20, 2023, without fine, applying oversight to administrative IT management while excluding judicial processes.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Judicial Processing Competence
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted guidelines in 2022 addressing the scope of supervisory authority competence over judicial bodies, providing examples of pure judicial acts (e.g., court orders) excluded from oversight versus administrative tasks (e.g., HR processing in courts).
process_and_outcome: Guidelines 4/2022 adopted on October 13, 2022, offer harmonized interpretation across EU, promoting consistent application and internal judicial compliance structures.

example_name: Schrems II Case - Facebook Ireland
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited, the European entity responsible for handling personal data of EU users, transferred data of hundreds of millions of users to the United States under the Privacy Shield framework. Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, filed complaints with the Irish Data Protection Commission arguing that US surveillance laws, including those enabling access by intelligence agencies such as the NSA, did not provide adequate safeguards for EU personal data. The case examined whether data transfers complied with GDPR requirements for third countries lacking adequacy decisions, highlighting concerns over public international law implications and extraterritorial reach of EU data protection rules. The processing involved behavioral advertising data and other personal information collected via Facebook services across the EU.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission referred the matter to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU). In July 2020, the CJEU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and imposed stricter requirements on Standard Contractual Clauses, requiring case-by-case assessments of third-country laws. Facebook suspended data transfers and implemented new measures.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Meta Platforms Privacy Shield Enforcement
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp in the EU, continued data transfers to the US post-Schrems II using Standard Contractual Clauses. The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an investigation into whether these transfers provided equivalent protection, considering US laws like FISA 702 and Executive Order 12333 that allow government access to non-US persons' data. The probe covered data of over 500 million EU users flowing to US servers for processing and storage.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion and ordered suspension of US data transfers. Meta appealed to the CJEU, which in 2023 upheld the decision. Meta implemented new EU-US Data Privacy Framework reliance.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Transfers
example_background: British Airways, operated by International Consolidated Airlines Group based in the UK (post-Brexit third country to EU), experienced a data breach affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details and personal data. The data was processed and transferred to servers in India and the US. The UK Information Commissioner's Office investigated compliance with international transfer rules, noting that post-Brexit, UK entities are non-EU controllers for EU data subjects when processing occurs outside adequacy frameworks.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a £20 million fine in 2020 (reduced from £183 million after appeal). British Airways remediated systems and enhanced transfer safeguards.

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers - CNIL Action
example_background: Multiple French websites using Google Analytics, provided by Google LLC in the US, transferred EU users' IP addresses and browsing data to US servers without adequate safeguards. The French CNIL declared such transfers unlawful after Schrems II, as Google's tool settings did not sufficiently anonymize data or account for US surveillance risks under public international law considerations.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CNIL ordered cessation of transfers and fined non-compliant sites up to €150,000. Google updated tools with IP anonymization and EU hosting options.

example_name: EU-US Data Privacy Framework Adoption by Microsoft
example_background: Microsoft Corporation, a US-based controller, processes personal data of EU customers through Azure cloud services and Office 365. Following invalidation of Privacy Shield, Microsoft participated in developing and self-certifying under the new EU-US Data Privacy Framework, committing to comply with principles ensuring equivalent protection for EU data transferred to the US, addressing CJEU concerns on surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the European Commission adopted adequacy decision for DPF. Microsoft certified compliance, enabling continued transfers without additional safeguards, audited by US FTC.

example_name: Amazon AWS GDPR Compliance Program
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS), operated by Amazon Web Services EMEA SARL in Luxembourg but with US parent, provides cloud services to EU controllers. AWS implemented Data Processing Addendum with SCCs supplemented by Technical and Organizational Measures, including EU data residency options and transparency reports on US government requests to demonstrate safeguards for international flows.
process_and_outcome: AWS achieved multiple GDPR compliance certifications and avoided major enforcement actions. In 2023, Irish DPC closed investigations without fines after verifying measures.

example_name: Max Schrems I - Safe Harbor Invalidation
example_background: Facebook transferred EU user data to the US under the 2000 Safe Harbor framework. Max Schrems challenged this before the Irish DPC, arguing US laws permitted indiscriminate access by public authorities, lacking equivalence to EU fundamental rights protections under public international law.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated Safe Harbor in 2015 (Schrems I). Led to Privacy Shield adoption, later also invalidated.

example_name: CJEU Ruling on Delisting Scope (Google v CNIL)
example_background: Following the 2014 Google Spain judgment, the French data protection authority CNIL ordered Google to delist links across all domain versions of its search engine worldwide, not just .fr or EU-specific versions. Google complied with EU delistings but resisted global delisting, arguing it exceeded the territorial scope of EU law. CNIL imposed fines for non-compliance with the worldwide order. The case examined the extraterritorial application of EU data protection rules to non-EU search activities and the interaction with intermediary liability exemptions.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the CJEU ruled that delisting applies to all EU country-specific domain versions but not to global .com results, balancing EU rights with freedom of information outside the EU. Google adjusted its practices accordingly, implementing geoblocking for EU users.
involved_parties: Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL)

example_name: eBay 'Liability for Listings' Case (L'Oréal v eBay)
example_background: L'Oréal filed complaints against eBay for trademark-infringing perfume and beauty product listings by third-party sellers on its platform. The case involved over 200 specific infringing listings where sellers offered counterfeit goods. eBay implemented notice-and-tTakedown systems via VeRO (Verified Rights Owner Program), but L'Oréal argued eBay had knowledge of infringement and failed to act proactively. The proceedings clarified the scope of intermediary liability exemptions for hosting providers under the E-Commerce Directive.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in 2011 that eBay qualifies as a hosting provider under Article 14 and is not liable absent specific knowledge of illegality, but must act expeditiously upon notification. eBay was not required to actively monitor all content but enhanced keyword filtering post-ruling.
involved_parties: UK and French courts (referring to CJEU)

example_name: YouTube Copyright Enforcement (Sony v Scarlet)
example_background: Music rights holders sued file-hosting service Scarlet Extended for facilitating P2P sharing of copyrighted music files. Scarlet provided no filtering or monitoring, and users shared millions of infringing files. The case tested hosting provider liability when services enable large-scale infringement without preventive measures.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2012 that under Article 14, intermediaries cannot rely on hosting exemption if they lack general monitoring obligations but must comply with specific injunctions. Scarlet ceased operations following injunctions and fines.

example_name: Facebook Illegality Notice Case (Eva Glawischnig-Piesczek v Facebook Ireland)
example_background: Austrian politician Eva Glawischnig-Piesczek obtained a court order against Facebook for defamatory posts insulting her integrity. Facebook removed the specific post in Austria upon notice but did not extend removal to identical content worldwide. The case addressed obligations of social platforms as information society services to remove illegal content beyond national borders.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, CJEU ruled that platforms must remove identical content globally upon valid court order if notified, but not materially differing content. Facebook implemented enhanced removal tools post-ruling.

example_name: UPC Telekabel Internet Blocking Order
example_background: Constantin Film and Wega requested UPC Telekabel, an Austrian ISP, to block access to websites streaming copyrighted films illegally. UPC argued as a mere conduit under Article 12, it should not be liable for third-party content accessed via its network.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2014 that ISPs can be ordered to block sites if proportionate, without triggering general monitoring obligations under Article 15. UPC implemented DNS/IP blocking measures.

example_name: CJEU on Active Knowledge (Papasavvas v eBay)
example_background: A Greek seller listed used watches on eBay described as 'new.' Buyers complained of misleading descriptions. The case examined when an online marketplace has 'actual knowledge' of illegal content, losing Article 14 protection.
process_and_outcome: CJEU clarified in 2017 that platforms with active involvement (e.g., optimization for higher bids) may not qualify for exemption. eBay refined its seller monitoring policies.

example_name: Google France v Louis Vuitton (Trademark Keywords)
example_background: Luxury brands sued Google for allowing advertisers to bid on their trademarks as AdWords keywords, leading to sponsored links for counterfeit or competitor sites. Google claimed conduit/ caching/hosting exemptions.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2010 that keyword advertising does not infringe if no confusion arises, and Google benefits from Article 12-14 protections absent active role in infringement. Google continued AdWords with disclosure policies.

example_name: MCF v Yandex Hosting Liability
example_background: A French company sought removal of copyright-infringing links from Yandex's Russian search engine. Yandex argued limited EU presence and intermediary status.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in 2023 that Article 14 applies to non-EU providers offering services in the EU, requiring expeditious removal upon notice. Yandex updated its geo-targeted compliance tools.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Facebook Fine 2023
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, established in Ireland as the main EU entity for Facebook services, processed personal data of EU users for behavioral advertising. The processing involved transferring data to the US without adequate safeguards after the invalidation of the Privacy Shield. The company's stable arrangements in Ireland directed the processing activities, which took place outside the EU but were linked to its EU establishment. The Irish DPC investigated complaints regarding data transfers and lack of appropriate transfer mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board adopted a binding decision directing the Irish DPC to impose a fine of 1.2 billion euros on Meta Platforms Ireland, the largest GDPR fine to date. Additional orders required suspension of US data transfers and implementation of data deletion measures.
involved_parties: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited (controller, EU establishment)

example_name: Amazon Europe Core Fine 2021
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., operating from Luxembourg as an EU establishment, processed personal data through its advertising services. The company used data from purchases and interactions for targeted advertising without a clear legal basis, combining data across services. Processing decisions were made from the Luxembourg entity, affecting EU consumers despite some data handling occurring outside the EU.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg CNPD imposed a fine of 746 million euros, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Amazon appealed the decision.
involved_parties: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. (controller, EU establishment)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland Fine 2021
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, the EU entity based in Dublin, processed personal data for its messaging service by sharing user data with Meta companies for advertising purposes.
